one_line_takeaway: Folding PGD adversarial training into both the SFT and GRPO stages of a medical VLM restores accuracy under PGD attack from ~25% to ~84% with only a ~3-point clean-accuracy cost, and explicit chain-of-thought reasoning further improves adversarial robustness.
key_finding: Standard fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-4B reaches high clean accuracy (95.43% Instruct, 94.25% Think overall on OmniMedVQA) but collapses under PGD attack to roughly 25-26% (26.07% Instruct, 25.14% Think). SafeMed-R1's adversarially fine-tuned models retain robustness: 84.45% (Think) and 82.27% (Instruct) accuracy under the same PGD attack — an absolute gain of ~58 percentage points over clean-only fine-tuning (the abstract rounds this to '59 percentage point improvement'; the body states ~58pp). This comes with only a modest clean-accuracy cost (95.43%->92.77% Instruct; 94.25%->91.47% Think), consistent with the known robustness–accuracy trade-off. A secondary finding: the clean-accuracy ordering (Instruct slightly > Think) REVERSES under attack, where Think > Instruct (84.45% vs 82.27%), supporting the claim that explicit GRPO-optimized reasoning aids robustness. For context, best zero-shot general VLM Qwen3-VL-4B is 78.00% clean / 59.50% under PGD, and best medical VLM MedGemma-4B is 68.75% clean / 54.38% under PGD.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SafeMed-R1, a hybrid defense framework for adversarial robustness of medical Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). Its own claimed novelty (Section 1, contributions list) is combining two defenses: (1) a training-time empirical defense that integrates Projected Gradient Descent adversarial training into BOTH the supervised fine-tuning and the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement-learning stages — termed AT-SFT + AT-GRPO — so that the reasoning process, not just the answer head, is hardened against worst-case multimodal perturbations; and (2) an inference-time certified defense via Randomized Smoothing (RS) giving an L2-norm certified radius adapted from Cohen et al. 2019. The authors position it as 'the first framework to combine adversarial reinforcement learning (AT-GRPO) with certified defenses (RS) for medical VQA.' Note: GRPO itself and RS itself are prior work (Med-R1/Shao et al.; Cohen et al.) that the paper cites and builds on, not its invention — the contribution is their integration under adversarial training for medical VQA and the empirical finding that explicit chain-of-thought reasoning improves robustness.
threat_model: White-box, first-order adversary who adds imperceptible L_p-bounded perturbations to the input medical image I (the paper focuses on the visual modality) to flip the VQA answer: formally, find δ_I in the threat set such that ExtractA(π(I+δ,Q)) != ExtractA(π(I,Q)). Attacks considered are FGSM, PGD (the main evaluated attack), and C&W (defined but, by the authors' own Limitations, not empirically tested). Perturbations are motivated as either malicious or arising from imaging-acquisition variation. Defense goal is both empirical robustness (adversarial training) and certified L2 robustness within a radius R_cert (randomized smoothing). Not covered: text/prompt attacks, multimodal joint attacks, and adaptive attacks targeting the reasoning process — explicitly acknowledged as out of scope.
limitations: Author-stated: evaluation is limited to PGD on the visual modality; C&W, multimodal/text-only, and adaptive attacks were not tested despite C&W/FGSM being formally defined. Certified robustness via randomized smoothing is described in full (Theorem 1, Algorithm 3, statistical guarantees in Appendix A.4) but — importantly — the Results section reports NO empirical certified-radius numbers; the authors note RS 'remains computationally expensive for large VLMs' and scaling it is an open problem, so the certified half of the 'hybrid' framework is proposed and analyzed but not empirically demonstrated with results. Reviewer-observed: training-time PGD is applied to input embeddings (ε=0.01) whereas the evaluation PGD is on the visual input, a mild train/test threat mismatch; PGD evaluation hyperparameters (ε, steps) are not clearly specified in the results, making the robustness numbers hard to reproduce or compare against the literature; the appendix 'convergence analysis' is largely a restatement of standard SFT/PPO/RS guarantees rather than new theory specific to AT-GRPO; and main results are reported for a single base model (Qwen3-VL-4B) despite four bases being listed.

datasets_used: OmniMedVQA (Hu et al. 2024), compiled from 73 public medical datasets
>88,000 image-question pairs (88,995 total; 71,333 train / 17,662 test, 80:20 split)
8 imaging modalities: CT, MRI, X-Ray, Ultrasound, Dermoscopy, Fundus Photography, OCT, Microscopy
quantitative_results: Standard (Clean FT) Qwen3-VL-4B overall clean accuracy: 95.43% Instruct, 94.25% Think
Standard FT under PGD collapses to ~25-26% overall (26.07% Instruct, 25.14% Think)
SafeMed-R1 (Adv FT) clean accuracy: 92.77% Instruct, 91.47% Think
SafeMed-R1 (Adv FT) under PGD: 84.45% Think, 82.27% Instruct overall
Robustness gain ~58 percentage points over clean-only FT (abstract rounds to 59pp)
Under-attack ordering reverses: Think 84.45% > Instruct 82.27% (clean: Instruct 95.43% > Think 94.25%)
Best zero-shot general VLM Qwen3-VL-4B: 78.00% clean / 59.50% under PGD
Best zero-shot medical VLM MedGemma-4B: 68.75% clean / 54.38% under PGD
No empirical certified-robustness (randomized smoothing radius) numbers are reported in the results
baselines_compared: Zero-shot general VLMs: BLIP-2, InstructBLIP, LLaVA, LLaMA-Adapter v2, MiniGPT-4, InternVL2, Qwen2-VL-2B/7B/72B, Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B/72B, Qwen3-VL-4B/8B
Zero-shot medical VLMs: LLaVA-Med, RadFM, Med-Flamingo, MedVInT, HuatuoGPT-Vision, HealthGPT, MedGemma-4B
Internal ablations: Clean fine-tuned (SFT / SFT+GRPO) vs Adversarial fine-tuned SafeMed-R1 (AT-SFT / AT-SFT+AT-GRPO); Instruct vs Think variants
